title: The Negotiation

jokes.text: A lot of people think that magic camp is just for kids. And that's why so many other people in my class were kids.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to defend magic camp as not just for kids
jokes.punchline: Inadvertently admits his class was mostly kids, undermining his defense
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael logic - trying to sound wise while accidentally proving the opposite point. Classic character-driven humor.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Magic camp is not just for kids. There were plenty of adults there.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I was the oldest one there by like three years.

jokes.text: And a little extra time after school.
jokes.setup: Michael listing requirements for magic camp after 'dream' and 'belief in magic'
jokes.punchline: The mundane scheduling detail deflates his grandiose description
jokes.explanation: Nice deflation of Michael's pretentious tone with practical reality. Economy works well.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dream. Belief in magic. And... a signed permission slip from a parent or legal guardian.

jokes.text: And now, Michael the Magic will attempt to escape from extreme bondage.
jokes.setup: Magic show announcement
jokes.punchline: Unintentionally sexual phrasing in workplace setting
jokes.explanation: Relies on accidental double entendre, which is fairly cheap but fits the cringe format.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I will be performing an incredible magic show for the office this Friday. I expect full attendance.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What kind of magic?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I will make things disappear and reappear. I'll have you all on the edge of your seats as I work my magic deep inside the box.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

jokes.text: I know how. He'd dislocate his shoulder and slip his arm out.
jokes.setup: Dwight asks if Michael can escape
jokes.punchline: Jim matter-of-factly describes the painful reality behind the 'magic'
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim - deflating Michael's showmanship with practical, medical reality. Perfect character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Do you think Michael could escape from this?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No. Michael would die.

jokes.text: So we shouldn't help you no matter how much you might beg and plead.
jokes.setup: Jim asking for clarification about the escape rules
jokes.punchline: Sets up Michael's inevitable failure and pleading
jokes.explanation: Jim's subtle setup for Michael's downfall shows great comedic plotting and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So if we don't escape in an hour, what happens?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, you're locked in here forever.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Forever?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, not forever. Just... a very long time.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How long?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I don't know. I didn't read the rules.

jokes.text: Help. Separately, on an unrelated note, if you happen to find a small brass key...
jokes.setup: Michael claiming he'll escape through magic and the magician's code
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself by asking for help and revealing he needs a key
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of expectations. The 'separately, on an unrelated note' delivery makes it even funnier. Great Michael moment.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm going to escape using magic and the magician's code.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, can someone help me? Does anyone have a key?

jokes.text: Early worm gets the worm. Another worm? Like, are they friends?
jokes.setup: Michael mangles the 'early bird gets the worm' saying
jokes.punchline: Kevin's innocent confusion about worm friendship
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moments for both - Michael's malapropism and Kevin's childlike interpretation. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what they say: the early bird gets the worm... and the second mouse gets the cheese.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Wait, why would the worm be friends with the bird?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You said the early bird gets the worm. That means they're together, right? So they must be friends.

jokes.text: Good, not great.
jokes.setup: Michael asks Pam to smell his breath
jokes.punchline: Pam's blunt, honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy of words and classic Pam honesty. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Pam, smell my breath.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Come on, just smell it.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Why would I want to do that?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I think it smells like cumin.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It smells like you've been eating garbage.

jokes.text: CFO, not his initials, common mistake
jokes.setup: Michael explaining the party importance
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily clarifies CFO isn't the person's initials
jokes.explanation: Vintage Michael - over-explaining something obvious while trying to sound knowledgeable. Great character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: The CFO is coming to our party tonight, and it's very important that we make a good impression.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: For those who don't know, CFO stands for Chief Financial Officer. Not... Carl F. Ontariosworth or anything like that.

jokes.text: Didn't expect to need a reason, so, let me think here. I don't know any of these people. It's an obligation. I don't like talking paper in my free time. Or in my work time. And... did I use the word 'pointless'?
jokes.setup: Jim asked why he doesn't want to go to the party
jokes.punchline: Increasingly honest and damning list of reasons, ending with meta-commentary
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim talking head - relatable, building absurdity, and great meta-awareness. Excellent character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know. I just... I don't really feel like going to a party where everyone from work is going to be there.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Plus, I've been to like five of these already this year, and they're all kind of the same.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Also, Dwight will definitely try to one-up me about something, and I'm just not in the mood for it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: And honestly? I'm tired of pretending to enjoy Michael's stories.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: But really, the main reason is... I'm getting tired of this show.

jokes.text: Really didn't give it any thought.
jokes.setup: Jim thanks Karen for inviting him
jokes.punchline: Karen's brutally honest response
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deflation of polite social conventions. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Thanks for inviting me.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: I didn't. Michael did.

jokes.text: That's what she said.
jokes.setup: Jan says 'Let's just blow this party off'
jokes.punchline: Michael's automatic 'that's what she said' response
jokes.explanation: Standard Michael running gag. Gets points for character consistency and quotability but lacks originality.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Let's just blow this party off

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said

jokes.text: Okay, let's just go to a motel... And, like, rip into each other like we did on that black sand beach in Jamaica
jokes.setup: Jan on speakerphone with office listening
jokes.punchline: Jan gets increasingly sexual while unaware others can hear
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe setup with Jan's obliviousness creating workplace awkwardness. Good escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Michael, are you there? I've been thinking about you all day.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: I can't wait to see you tonight. I want to do things to you that would make your head spin.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: I'm getting hot just thinking about it. What are you wearing?

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Tell me you want me, Michael. Tell me right now.

jokes.text: My brother just unloaded the Jet Skis and he kinda took a bath, so... We're gonna go get hammered.
jokes.setup: Roy explaining why he can't come to the bar
jokes.punchline: Accidentally uses 'took a bath' (lost money) then immediately plans to get drunk about it
jokes.explanation: Good Roy character moment - financial problems leading to drinking. Sets up later payoffs.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: I can't go to the bar tonight, man. I took a bath on that used truck I bought.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? Forget it. I'm getting drunk.

jokes.text: Okay, well, we're going to a bar.
jokes.setup: Roy says they're going to get hammered
jokes.punchline: Jim's understated correction of Roy's dramatic language
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim - deflating dramatic language with mundane reality. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: We're going to get hammered.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Or we could just have a couple beers.

jokes.text: And, don't call me Pamie.
jokes.setup: Pam's talking head about being more direct
jokes.punchline: Immediately demonstrates her new assertiveness on a trivial point
jokes.explanation: Great character development moment - Pam practicing assertiveness on something small. Good structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I've decided to be more direct and assertive. No more beating around the bush.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim, those are my Cheetos.

jokes.text: You're dressed exactly like the servants.
jokes.setup: Jim and Michael at the fancy party
jokes.punchline: Jim points out they're inappropriately dressed
jokes.explanation: Good cringe setup for the fancy party fish-out-of-water scenario.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, we're wearing khakis and polos to a black-tie event.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So? I think we look sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Everyone else is in tuxedos.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't get the memo.

jokes.text: I don't think your's will fit me. I don't care.
jokes.setup: Michael wants to switch shirts with Jim
jokes.punchline: Michael's desperate willingness to wear an ill-fitting shirt
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy setup showing Michael's social desperation.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jim, Jim, Jim. I need to borrow your shirt.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What? Why would you need my shirt?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Because I spilled coffee on mine and I have a meeting with corporate in five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, your shirt is twice the size of mine. It's not going to fit.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I don't care. Give it to me.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's fine. I'll just... I'll make it work.

jokes.text: Actually, it's polite to arrive early. And, smart. Only really good friends show up early. Ergo de facto, go to a party early become a really good friend.
jokes.setup: Someone comments on arriving early to Roy's party
jokes.punchline: Dwight's nonsensical logic chain with fake Latin
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - pseudo-intellectual reasoning with fake authority. The 'ergo de facto' is especially good.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, of course I arrived early. It's basic mathematics. The early bird gets the worm, but the second mouse gets the cheese. Mice, birds, they're all just trying to survive. And survival is 90% about showing up before everyone else. Which is why I always arrive at least 45 minutes early to any social gathering. It's called 'Primus Arrivis,' a little Latin I picked up. Means 'first arrival.' The Romans knew what they were doing.

jokes.text: No, it's not. It's my bowl.
jokes.setup: Kevin says the potato salad is from both of them
jokes.punchline: Angela's petty correction about bowl ownership
jokes.explanation: Classic Angela pettiness. Perfect character moment with great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: The potato salad is from both of us.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: It's from me. That's my bowl.

jokes.text: Sun beating down on the mayonnaise... Just... you never know.
jokes.setup: Kevin suggesting they keep the cover on the potato salad
jokes.punchline: Kevin's ominous warning about potential food poisoning
jokes.explanation: Good Kevin moment - accidentally creating tension about something mundane. The trailing off works well.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: We should keep the cover on the potato salad.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: If someone eats it without the cover, they're gonna get food poisoning. Bad food poisoning.

jokes.text: It's complicated. I would appreciate some space on this.
jokes.setup: Asked if he and Stacey set a wedding date
jokes.punchline: Kevin treats a simple question like a deeply personal crisis
jokes.explanation: Great Kevin character moment - overly dramatic response to casual inquiry. Very quotable phrasing.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: A wedding date? Do you know what you're asking me? That's... that's a huge decision. I mean, what if I pick a date and then later there's a better date? What if it rains? What if it's too sunny? Stacey wants June, but what if June is bad this year?

jokes.text: That's feces.
jokes.setup: Someone mentions the line on top of shrimp
jokes.punchline: Creed's blunt, disgusting fact at a party
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed - inappropriate truth-telling at the worst moment. Maximum economy.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: You know, I've eaten things that would make a billy goat puke. I once ate a whole wheel of cheese. I'm not even sure it was cheese.

jokes.text: I am taking a calculated risk. What's the upside? I overcome my nausea, fall deeply in love, babies, normalcy, no more self-loathing. Downside, I, uh- date Michael Scott publicly. And collapse into myself like a dying star.
jokes.setup: Jan's talking head about dating Michael
jokes.punchline: The dramatic escalation to 'collapse into myself like a dying star'
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation and metaphor. The 'dying star' comparison is both dramatic and scientifically apt. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: I mean, dating Michael is like... it starts out fine, and then it just gets worse and worse. Like, first it's small things, you know, he forgets to call. Then he lies about where he was. Then he's screaming at you in a Chili's. And then... I don't know, you just collapse into yourself like a dying star.

jokes.text: That's what she said. Oh, my God. What am I saying? I love this woman!
jokes.setup: Jan asks 'Why is this so hard?' about signing papers
jokes.punchline: Michael's automatic response followed by horrified self-realization
jokes.explanation: Good twist on the running gag - Michael's immediate regret adds character depth to the automatic response.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: Do you ever watch Battlestar Galactica? No? Then, you're an idiot.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to make conversation at the party
jokes.punchline: Immediately insults the host's wife over TV preferences
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight social skills - zero to insult in record time. Great character consistency and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Your wife has terrible taste in television. The Bachelor is for people with no ambition or critical thinking skills.

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: That's... my wife you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Exactly my point.

jokes.text: Jan and I are lovers.
jokes.setup: Michael introducing Jan to his boss
jokes.punchline: Uses the most awkward possible word choice
jokes.explanation: 'Lovers' is the worst possible word choice for this situation. Perfect Michael cringe moment.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jan, this is my boss, Jan. Jan, this is my boss.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We're... we're very close. Very intimate.

jokes.text: 'Like butter.' Mike Myers, SNL
jokes.setup: Asked how the merger went
jokes.punchline: Michael gives a random pop culture reference as if it's profound
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael random reference humor. Fits character but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: How did the merger go? It's like when Forrest Gump said life is like a box of chocolates. You never know what you're gonna get. That's mergers.

jokes.text: And, there's one in anger management.
jokes.setup: Karen explaining what happened to Stamford employees
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions someone in anger management as an afterthought
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor - treating anger management as just another job outcome. Nice economy.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: Most of the Stamford employees are being transferred here to Scranton.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: And we're going to merge the two branches together.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: Oh, and Ryan's in anger management now, so that's... good.

jokes.text: I could go for an appe-tease-er, yep.
jokes.setup: Host asking if anyone wants anything
jokes.punchline: Michael's unnecessary pronunciation emphasis
jokes.explanation: Standard Michael trying-too-hard moment. Fits character but not very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yes, I would like a bottle of water, please. And not just any water — I want it to be from a spring. A spring that I can pronounce: Perrier.

jokes.text: Dwight, wow- that's not appropriate. I don't know. It's a common question.
jokes.setup: Dwight asks about house cost
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects him then Dwight doubles down
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamic - Michael trying to be appropriate while Dwight remains clueless.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: How much did your house cost?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's way too much. I could get a house like that for $200,000.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, you couldn't, Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yes, I could. I know a guy.

jokes.text: Here is to Mr. Iacocca and his failed experiment, The DeLorean.
jokes.setup: David making a toast with expensive scotch
jokes.punchline: Confidently attributes DeLorean to wrong automotive executive
jokes.explanation: Good joke about executive confidence being wrong. Works on knowledge level - funny if you get it.

dialogue.speaker: David Brent
dialogue.line: I'd like to propose a toast with this rather expensive scotch. You know, it reminds me of the time Lee Iacocca invented the DeLorean.

jokes.text: I can read you like a book. Oh, yeah? You can't keep anything form me.
jokes.setup: Roy boasts about reading Pam after winning card game
jokes.punchline: Dramatic irony since Pam is about to reveal a major secret
jokes.explanation: Excellent dramatic irony - Roy's confidence is about to be completely shattered. Great setup.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: I know Pam like the back of my hand. I can read her like a book.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Actually, Roy, I'm pregnant.

jokes.text: I run a small fake ID company from my car with a laminating machine that I swiped from the sheriff's station.
jokes.setup: Creed being called 'the man'
jokes.punchline: Casually confesses to multiple felonies
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed - casual criminality delivered matter-of-factly. Great escalation and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm the man around here. I've been here the longest, I know where all the bodies are buried. Literally. I know where they are.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: I've done things you people wouldn't believe. Tax evasion, identity theft, I once bit a man's ear off in Saigon. But I got better.

jokes.text: These studs are way too far apart.
jokes.setup: Dwight examining the house structure
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate home inspection at a party
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight social inappropriateness. Fits character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This foundation is poorly constructed. I'm going to need to examine the load-bearing walls.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, you're a guest at a party, not a structural engineer.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A Schrute is always prepared. Plus, I noticed the drywall has water damage on the east side.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: How is that even visible from here?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A trained eye sees what others cannot. Now, if you'll excuse me, I need to inspect the attic for mold and rodent droppings.

jokes.text: They don't allow kids at Sandals. They are- are persona non grata.
jokes.setup: Michael suggesting Christmas vacation
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily uses Latin term for 'kids not allowed'
jokes.explanation: Michael trying to sound sophisticated about a resort policy. Typical character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We're going to have an amazing Christmas vacation. It's going to be adults only. No kids. What's that Latin term? Oh yeah, 'No rugrats.'

jokes.text: No means please don't. Slam me up against the wall, right here.
jokes.setup: Michael and Jan's bathroom encounter
jokes.punchline: Jan's contradictory signals and inappropriate venue choice
jokes.explanation: Peak Jan dysfunction - mixed signals and inappropriate behavior. Very uncomfortable and funny.

jokes.text: Hey, who makes this chair? I don't know. It was here when I was born.
jokes.setup: Dwight asking about furniture
jokes.punchline: Host's absurd response about chair predating her existence
jokes.explanation: Absurd response that treats furniture as eternal. Good escalation of the inspection running gag.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Where did you get this chair?

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: Oh, this chair? It's been in my family for generations. My great-great-grandfather sat in it.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: How is that possible? You're only 32.

jokes.text: What do you know?
jokes.setup: Rachel says she doesn't know about the chair
jokes.punchline: Dwight's frustrated, inappropriate response to the host
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Dwight's frustration. Economical and character-appropriate rudeness.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: I don't know about the chair.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, that's because you're an idiot.

jokes.text: Have you dated like every guy here? Wow. Okay. You got me. I so got you. So, none of them. Of course not. I mean, you're kind of like, my first.
jokes.setup: Jim gets increasingly jealous about Karen's dating history
jokes.punchline: Karen reveals she was lying about all of it - Jim is her first
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and character development. Karen's delight at fooling Jim is perfectly played.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, so all those guys you told me about... the banker, the guy from yoga, that musician...

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: I made them all up.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What? Why would you—

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: Because you kept asking about my exes, and I didn't want you to think I was inexperienced. So I just... invented some guys.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So you're saying...

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: You're actually my first serious relationship, yeah.

jokes.text: Rachel thinks that I brought homemade potato salad. And I just picked it up at the supermarket. It's funny, I wish I could make potato salad that good. It's just potatoes and mayonnaise.
jokes.setup: Kevin's talking head about the potato salad mix-up
jokes.punchline: Kevin's innocent amazement at store-bought food being better than homemade
jokes.explanation: Sweet Kevin moment - genuine confusion about cooking. The 'just potatoes and mayonnaise' shows his simplistic thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: I don't understand. Store-bought is better than homemade? That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: My potato salad is... it's a recipe that's been passed down through my family for generations.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: But I guess... I guess Kraft just knows something my family doesn't.

jokes.text: I found some termite damage in a crawlspace and some structural flaws in the foundation so all in all... it was a pretty fun cocktail party.
jokes.setup: Dwight listing house problems
jokes.punchline: Concludes this constituted a fun party experience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - his idea of party fun is finding structural problems. Great callback and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have termites in the east wing, the septic tank is backed up, and the former owners left a pentagram in the basement. But you know what? I consider this a fun party experience.

jokes.text: I kissed Jim. What? He told me how he felt, and I guess I had feelings too, and we kissed.
jokes.setup: Pam deciding to be honest with Roy
jokes.punchline: The bombshell revelation delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Major plot revelation delivered with Pam's characteristic understatement. Great character development and callback.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Roy, I need to be honest with you about something.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I slept with Jim.

jokes.text: Damn Jet Skis!
jokes.setup: Roy's relationship ending
jokes.punchline: Blames the jet skis for his problems
jokes.explanation: Good callback - Roy blaming material things instead of relationship issues. Economical and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: It's the jet skis, man. Everything was fine until the jet skis.

jokes.text: Our first fight.
jokes.setup: Jan storms out after their bathroom encounter
jokes.punchline: Michael's oddly romantic interpretation of dysfunction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael - finding relationship milestones in disasters. Great economy and character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? I think that went really well.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: She's upset, which means she cares. That's love.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dysfunction is just passion without a manual.

jokes.text: I want the house, Jan! I want the picket fence. I want the ketchup fights, and the tickling, and the giggling.
jokes.setup: Michael pleading with Jan
jokes.punchline: His childish vision of domestic bliss, especially 'ketchup fights'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael romanticism - his domestic fantasy is like a sitcom from the 1950s. 'Ketchup fights' is especially absurd and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jan, come on. We could have a really great life together. We could have a house with a big yard, and a hot tub. And we could have little Michael Juniors running around.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And on Saturdays, we could have ketchup fights in the kitchen.

jokes.text: Don't break up, you guys. You're great together.
jokes.setup: Michael and Jan's dysfunctional relationship moment
jokes.punchline: Dwight's completely tone-deaf encouragement of toxic relationship
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight misreading the situation. His sincerity makes the dysfunction even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Michael, Jan is testing you. It's what strong women do. My father always said, 'A woman who yells at you in public is a woman who respects you.' You should be honored.

jokes.text: Jet Ski money? All of it. I'm gonna kill Jim Halpert.
jokes.setup: Roy spent all his money on bribing the police
jokes.punchline: Threatens Jim with murder over financial problems caused by confession
jokes.explanation: Good callback integration and character-appropriate response. Roy's logic of blaming Jim for everything works for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: You know what, Jim? I spent all my money bribing the cops, and now I'm broke. And it's YOUR fault for making me confess!

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: I'm gonna kill you, man. I'm serious.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Roy, I didn't make you do anything—

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Shut up! You think this is funny? I'm gonna murder you!

tmdb_overview: Roy paced in the parking lot outside Dunder-Mifflin and then charged the building looking for Jim. When he spots Jim and rushed him, only to get pepper sprayed in the eyes by Dwight, who managed to spray everybody around him, as well.
insight: Cringe-driven episode leans on character moments over joke density at 71.9.
